nativeRange.habitat: Shallow coastal to deep outer-reef drop-offs, typically at depths of 0–15 m (range 0–50 m); tends to hover close to coral or rubble, often solitary or in small loosely scattered groups

diet.notes: Generally considered an unfussy micropredator that has been reported to readily accept most prepared aquarium foods, including mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, finely chopped meaty foods (krill, shrimp, squid, clam, mussel), and quality marine flake or pellet preparations. Has been documented preying on small crustaceans and nuisance bristleworms in the display. Generally advised to feed 2–3 small meals per day with varied, pigment-rich foods to help maintain its purple-and-yellow coloration.

summary: A small, vivid purple-and-yellow Western Pacific dottyback that is generally considered beginner-friendly and hardy, but is notably aggressive and territorial. Best kept singly in a tank with ample rockwork.
careNotes:
The Bicolor Dottyback is generally considered one of the hardiest beginner-level saltwater fish — it adapts readily to captive foods, tolerates a wide parameter window, and is widely available in the trade. Despite its small size, it has been widely reported to display a notably outsized attitude: TFH Magazine describes it as having "a grouper-size attitude" that will defend its rockwork territory against fish two to three times its size, and have been known to nip fins of much larger tankmates. The careLevel rating here reflects its physical hardiness; the aggressive temperament is a separate dimension that should be planned for in tankmate selection.

Conspecific aggression is reported as severe — keep one per tank, and avoid keeping it with the similarly shaped/colored Royal Gramma (Gramma loreto), which it can be mistaken for and fights to death. It should also not be combined with other dottybacks. Reef compatibility is generally considered good with corals and most sessile invertebrates, but the species has been reliably reported to prey on small ornamental shrimp and other mobile micro-inverts, so it is rated reef-safe with caution.

A tightly-sealed lid is generally advised — reefapp.net and other sources note the species has been reported to jump from open tanks. Plenty of live rock with numerous caves, crevices, and overhangs is required to give it a defendable home territory; the minimum tank size used here (150 L, ~40 US gal) goes above the universal retail-source 30-gal recommendation to account for both the territorial behavior and the need to give more timid tankmates space outside its claimed zone.

Cross-source spread: adult size — LiveAquaria reports 3 in (7.6 cm), TFH Magazine 2¾ in (7 cm), FishBase 7.0 cm TL, reefapp 7 cm; converged on ~7 cm max. Temperature — LiveAquaria states 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C); FishBase wild observations 25.8–29.3°C; reefapp "close to 26°C"; averaged to 23–27°C. Salinity — LiveAquaria 1.020–1.025 SG; reefapp ~1.026 SG; standard reef range 1.020–1.025 retained. Minimum tank — LiveAquaria 30 gal (~114 L); reefapp 50 L; biased upward to 150 L per the tank-size special rule given the aggressive territorial behavior and need for ample rockwork.

Nomenclature note: FishBase currently lists Pictichromis paccagnellorum (Axelrod, 1973) as the accepted name; the trade and most aquarium literature still use Pictichromis paccagnellae (and the older Pseudochromis paccagnellae synonym).
breedingNotes: Has been reported to be bred in captivity, though commercial availability of captive-bred specimens remains less common than for some other dottyback species. As with congeners, the male is generally expected to prepare a cave or crevice and court a female who deposits an egg ball inside. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging due to the small size of the larvae.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: Used for confirmed scientific name (Pictichromis paccagnellae), care level (Easy/beginner), temperament (Semi-Aggressive — defends territory against fish 2–3x its size), reef-safe (Yes), diet (carnivore — meaty foods, frozen, bristleworm predator), max size (3 in), minimum tank (30 gal), water parameters (72–78°F, pH 8.1–8.4, SG 1.020–1.025, dKH 8–12), family Pseudochromidae, and the bicolor (anterior purple, posterior yellow) description.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Pseudochromidae, order Ovalentaria/misc), accepted scientific name Pictichromis paccagnellorum (Axelrod, 1973) — paccagnellae retained as primary name to match trade usage and original description, max length 7.0 cm TL, depth 0–50 m (typically 0–15 m), wild temperature 25.8–29.3°C, native distribution (Indonesia: Java and Sulawesi; Vanuatu; Palau), IUCN Least Concern.

additional.name: TFH Magazine
additional.notes: Third source for temperament (extremely aggressive/territorial, 'grouper-size attitude', will fight conspecifics to death and may nip fins of larger tankmates), confirmed beginner-hardiness despite aggression, max size 7 cm (2¾ in), reef-safe with caution (hunts small crustaceans), readily accepts captive foods, has been bred in captivity. Source for the alsoKnownAs list (royal dottyback, royal pseudochromis, bicolor cichlops, false gramma).

additional.name: reefapp.net
additional.notes: Fourth source for max size (7 cm), temperature (~26°C), salinity (~1.026 SG), threat-to-shrimp/crustacean rating (reef safe with caution), and the jumping behavior that justifies the escapeRisk: high rating and recommendation for a tightly-sealed lid.

fish.breedingNotes: Cave-spawning species; male prepares a hiding site and courts the female, who deposits an egg ball inside the cave. Has been bred in captivity but captive-bred specimens are less widely available than for some other dottybacks. Raising fry is considered moderately challenging.
